title: Political Metabolic Disease: When Civilizations Collapse Under Their Own Weight

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
There's a recurring pattern in the rise and fall of civilizations that mirrors metabolic disease in biology. Empires begin with nothing — forced into discipline, innovation, and hard work because failure means extinction. The virtuous ones rise. They accumulate power, wealth, and prestige. They become universally admired. And then the decay begins, not from external threats, but from internal excess.

The mechanism is what Saylor calls political metabolic disease: everything gets too big. Big government, big charity, big banks, big education, big medicine, big foreign policy, big defense. Each expansion requires funding, which requires either taxation, inflation, confiscation, or regulatory impairment of property rights. Nature imposes hard limits on this — there are no 100-ton mammals on land because beyond the elephant's mass, a creature collapses under its own weight. Civilizations face the same constraint, but the feedback loop is slower and easier to ignore.

The historical pattern is remarkably consistent. Marcus Aurelius leaves Rome to Commodus. Napoleon inherits revolutionary France and decides French values must be spread to the frozen Russian steppe. The British Empire reaches too far. In every case, grandiose ambitions exceed the empire's capacity to fund and sustain them. The terminal symptom is always the same: the empire can no longer pay its soldiers — or in modern terms, it can no longer sustain its dependent class of public sector workers whose wages fall behind real inflation.

The irony is that the places most universally admired are typically the ones furthest along in decay. Paris, London, New York, California — they're so rich that every social wrong seems correctable, every entitlement seems affordable. First comes the universal program, then the mandate, then confiscation to fund the mandate, then social collapse. What starts as generosity ends as compulsion.

The counter-examples are instructive. Singapore rose from poverty by implementing rational tax policies, free trade, English as a communication protocol, and rule of law. Dubai built prosperity in a desert with no fresh water, no trees, and no natural advantages beyond ambition and smart policy. The lesson is that civilizational health requires humility and constraint — the willingness to remain small and disciplined rather than expand into every conceivable domain of human welfare.
